original_abstract:
This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the development, institutional landscape, and thematic priorities of higher education research in Europe. While the field has grown significantly since the 1960s—transitioning from a marginal activity to a visible area of expertise—it remains characterized by fuzzy boundaries between academic research, policy consultancy, and administrative practice. Unlike the more standardized and teaching-embedded model found in the United States, European higher education research is scattered across diverse disciplinary affiliations and institutional settings, ranging from university-based centers to nationally sponsored independent institutes.

The analysis highlights a notable shift in research focus over the last few decades, moving from a primary interest in teaching and learning toward macro-level issues of governance, management, and system differentiation. Furthermore, the rise of "Europeanization" through the Bologna Process and various EU-funded initiatives has significantly increased the demand for international comparative studies and "evidence-based" policy analysis. Using Germany as a specific case study, the paper illustrates the challenges of visibility for non-English language research and the growing role of "higher education professionals" in knowledge generation. Ultimately, the author suggests that the field is currently navigating a tension between seeking high-level academic recognition and meeting the practical, often instrumental, needs of policy-makers and practitioners in an era of increasing complexity.
humanized_abstract:
This paper aims to give an overview of the developments, structures and focuses of higher education research in Europe. Based on sociological analysis, it takes a glance at the long way that higher education research had to travel from being a marginal scientific discipline to becoming an accepted area of science. Today higher education research is still characterized by a number of unclear lines between scientific work, policy-oriented consultancy and administrative practice – in contrast to for example the USA, where the lines between science and practice are more clearly defined, and higher education research is embedded in a regular academic structure, far less marginally than in Europe. European higher education research is still a highly inhomogeneous discipline - characterized by a large number of different disciplines and locations: research centers affiliated to individual universities and departments as well as a large number of independent national research institutions.

According to the analysis, the focus of research in higher education has undergone a paradigm shift in recent years. Researchers are no longer as concerned with the act of teaching and learning, and are now preoccupied with a range of issues including governance, management and institutional diversity. The increase in Europeanization as a result of the Bologna Process and EU-funded projects has also heightened the demand for comparative research as well as evidence-based policy analysis. The case study of Germany is used to examine the issues of visibility of research conducted in languages other than English and the new roles of "higher education professionals" in knowledge production. The author argues that the HE field is caught in a world of contradictions, where there is a struggle between achieving high-level academic standing, and fulfilling the increasingly complex and often instrumental demands of policymakers and practitioners.
